title: Applied Graph Theory with Matrix Representations + Exercises
description: A serious MathLesson pilot lesson on applied graph theory: graph models, adjacency matrices, degree matrices, walk counting, Laplacians, incidence matrices, directed graphs, reachability, spectral intuition, centrality, and exercises.

sections.label: 1. Graphs as models
sections.title: Graphs as mathematical models
sections.content:
Applied Graph Theory with Matrix Representations

A graph is a mathematical model for pairwise relationships. The vertices represent entities and the edges represent connections, interactions, constraints, transitions, or dependencies.

Graph
A graph is usually written $G=(V,E)$, where $V$ is a finite set of vertices and $E$ is a set of edges. For an undirected graph, an edge is an unordered pair $\{u,v\}$. For a directed graph, an edge is an ordered pair $(u,v)$.
ApplicationVerticesEdgesTypical question
Transportationcities or stationsroads, rails, flightsWhat is the shortest route?
Social networkspeople or accountsfriendship, follows, messagesWho is central?
Markov modelsstatespossible transitionsWhat is the long-run distribution?
Computationtasks or modulesdependenciesWhat order is feasible?
A small undirected graph
flowchart LR
  A((A)) --- B((B))
  A --- C((C))
  B --- D((D))
  C --- D
  D --- E((E))

This lesson emphasizes the matrix viewpoint: a graph is not only a picture. It can be encoded, computed with, and analyzed using linear algebra.

Basic object
$$G=(V,E),\qquad V=\{1,2,\ldots,n\}.$$
If a simple undirected graph has vertices $V=\{1,2,3,4\}$ and edges $E=\{\{1,2\},\{2,3\},\{3,4\}\}$, how many edges does it have?

sections.label: 2. Adjacency matrix
sections.title: The adjacency matrix
sections.content:
The Adjacency Matrix

The most direct matrix representation of a graph is the adjacency matrix. It records which vertices are connected.

Adjacency matrix
For a graph with vertices $1,\ldots,n$, the adjacency matrix $A=(a_{ij})$ is defined by
$$a_{ij}=\begin{cases}1,&\text{if vertex }i\text{ is adjacent to vertex }j,\\0,&\text{otherwise.}\end{cases}$$
For a weighted graph, $a_{ij}$ may be the edge weight instead of only $0$ or $1$.
Graph for the adjacency example
flowchart LR
  1((1)) --- 2((2))
  1 --- 3((3))
  2 --- 3
  3 --- 4((4))

For the graph above, with vertex order $(1,2,3,4)$, the adjacency matrix is

$$A=\begin{pmatrix} 0&1&1&0\\ 1&0&1&0\\ 1&1&0&1\\ 0&0&1&0 \end{pmatrix}.$$
Symmetry test
The adjacency matrix of an undirected graph is symmetric: $A=A^T$. A directed graph usually has a nonsymmetric adjacency matrix.
Graph typeMatrix feature
simple undirected$A$ is symmetric and diagonal entries are usually zero
directed$A$ may be nonsymmetric
weightedentries store weights rather than only $0$ or $1$
self-loops alloweddiagonal entries may be nonzero
In the displayed matrix, what is $a_{34}$?

sections.label: 3. Degrees
sections.title: Degree matrix and degree vector
sections.content:
Degrees and the Degree Matrix

The degree of a vertex is the number of edges incident to it. In an undirected simple graph, the degree of vertex $i$ is the sum of row $i$ of the adjacency matrix.

Degree from adjacency matrix
$$d_i=\sum_{j=1}^n a_{ij}.$$

For the example graph from the previous section,

$$A=\begin{pmatrix} 0&1&1&0\\ 1&0&1&0\\ 1&1&0&1\\ 0&0&1&0 \end{pmatrix},\qquad (d_1,d_2,d_3,d_4)=(2,2,3,1).$$
Degree matrix
The degree matrix $D$ is the diagonal matrix with vertex degrees on the diagonal:
$$D=\operatorname{diag}(d_1,\ldots,d_n).$$
$$D=\begin{pmatrix} 2&0&0&0\\ 0&2&0&0\\ 0&0&3&0\\ 0&0&0&1 \end{pmatrix}.$$
Handshake lemma
For an undirected graph, the sum of all vertex degrees is twice the number of edges:
$$\sum_{i=1}^n d_i=2|E|.$$
A graph has degree vector $(2,2,3,1)$. How many edges does it have?

sections.label: 4. Walks and powers
sections.title: Walks from powers of the adjacency matrix
sections.content:
Walks from Matrix Powers

The adjacency matrix does more than store connections. Its powers count walks.

Walk-counting theorem
For an unweighted graph with adjacency matrix $A$, the entry $(A^k)_{ij}$ equals the number of walks of length $k$ from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$.
Matrix multiplication mechanism
$$(A^2)_{ij}=\sum_{m=1}^n a_{im}a_{mj}.$$

This formula sums over all possible intermediate vertices $m$. A product $a_{im}a_{mj}$ contributes $1$ precisely when $i\to m\to j$ is a valid two-step walk.

Example
If vertex $1$ is adjacent to vertices $2$ and $3$, and both $2$ and $3$ are adjacent to vertex $1$, then there are two length-$2$ walks from $1$ back to $1$: $1\to2\to1$ and $1\to3\to1$.
Two-step walk through an intermediate vertex
flowchart LR
  I["start i"] --> M["intermediate m"]
  M --> J["end j"]
  A["sum over all m"] -.-> M
Matrix expressionGraph meaning
$A_{ij}$edge from $i$ to $j$
$(A^2)_{ij}$number of two-step walks from $i$ to $j$
$(A^k)_{ij}$number of $k$-step walks from $i$ to $j$
$\operatorname{tr}(A^3)$closed walks of length $3$
In a triangle graph with vertices $1,2,3$, how many length-$2$ walks go from vertex $1$ back to vertex $1$?

sections.label: 5. Laplacian
sections.title: The graph Laplacian
sections.content:
The Graph Laplacian

The graph Laplacian is one of the most important matrix objects in applied graph theory. It compares a value at a vertex with values at neighboring vertices.

Combinatorial Laplacian
For an undirected graph, the combinatorial graph Laplacian is
$$L=D-A,$$
where $D$ is the degree matrix and $A$ is the adjacency matrix.

For a function $f:V\to\mathbb{R}$, represented as a vector $f=(f_1,\ldots,f_n)^T$,

$$(Lf)_i=d_i f_i-\sum_{j\sim i}f_j=\sum_{j\sim i}(f_i-f_j).$$
Interpretation
$Lf$ is small at vertex $i$ when $f_i$ is close to the neighboring values. This is why the graph Laplacian appears in smoothing, diffusion, clustering, electrical networks, and spectral graph theory.
Example path graph $1-2-3$
$$A=\begin{pmatrix}0&1&0\\1&0&1\\0&1&0\end{pmatrix},\quad D=\begin{pmatrix}1&0&0\\0&2&0\\0&0&1\end{pmatrix},\quad L=\begin{pmatrix}1&-1&0\\-1&2&-1\\0&-1&1\end{pmatrix}.$$
Path graph for the Laplacian example
flowchart LR
  1((1)) --- 2((2)) --- 3((3))
For the path graph $1-2-3$, what is the middle diagonal entry $L_{22}$?

sections.label: 6. Incidence matrices
sections.title: Incidence matrices and orientation
sections.content:
Incidence Matrices and Orientation

An incidence matrix records how edges touch vertices. For many applications, we orient each edge arbitrarily and record signs.

Oriented incidence matrix
Let $B$ be an $n\times m$ matrix, with one row per vertex and one column per edge. For edge $e$ oriented from tail $u$ to head $v$,
$$B_{ie}=\begin{cases}-1,&i=u,\\1,&i=v,\\0,&\text{otherwise.}\end{cases}$$
Oriented path
flowchart LR
  1((1)) -->|e1| 2((2))
  2 -->|e2| 3((3))

For the oriented path $1\to2\to3$,

$$B=\begin{pmatrix} -1&0\\ 1&-1\\ 0&1 \end{pmatrix}.$$
Laplacian factorization
For an undirected graph with an arbitrary orientation assigned to each edge,
$$L=BB^T.$$
This factorization connects graph theory with gradients, divergences, flows, and energy minimization.
Energy identity
$$f^T L f=\sum_{\{i,j\}\in E}(f_i-f_j)^2.$$
For the oriented edge $1 o2$, what is the entry in the row for vertex 1 in that edge column?

sections.label: 7. Directed graphs
sections.title: Directed graphs and transition matrices
sections.content:
Directed Graphs and Transition Matrices

Directed graphs encode asymmetric relationships: one-way roads, hyperlinks, state transitions, precedence constraints, and causal influence.

Directed adjacency matrix
For a directed graph, $a_{ij}=1$ means there is a directed edge from $i$ to $j$. The matrix need not be symmetric.
Small directed graph
flowchart LR
  A((A)) --> B((B))
  A --> C((C))
  B --> C
  C --> A

With vertex order $(A,B,C)$, the directed adjacency matrix is

$$A_G=\begin{pmatrix} 0&1&1\\ 0&0&1\\ 1&0&0 \end{pmatrix}.$$
Transition matrix
A transition matrix $P=(p_{ij})$ is a nonnegative matrix with row sums equal to $1$. It represents transition probabilities from one state to another.
Example transition matrix
$$P=\begin{pmatrix} 0&1/2&1/2\\ 0&0&1\\ 1&0&0 \end{pmatrix}.$$
Bridge to Markov processes
A directed graph tells which transitions are possible. A transition matrix tells how likely those transitions are.
In the displayed transition matrix, what is $p_{13}$?

sections.label: 8. Reachability
sections.title: Reachability and transitive closure
sections.content:
Reachability and Transitive Closure

A vertex $j$ is reachable from vertex $i$ if there is a path from $i$ to $j$. Matrix powers encode reachability because $A^k$ counts walks of length $k$.

Reachability matrix
For a graph with $n$ vertices, one way to detect reachability is to inspect
$$I+A+A^2+\cdots+A^{n-1}.$$
A positive $(i,j)$ entry indicates that $j$ is reachable from $i$ by a walk of length at most $n-1$.
Reachability in a directed acyclic graph
flowchart LR
  A((A)) --> B((B))
  A --> C((C))
  B --> D((D))
  C --> D
  D --> E((E))
Example
In the diagram above, $E$ is reachable from $A$ through multiple paths: $A\to B\to D\to E$ and $A\to C\to D\to E$.
ConceptMatrix viewAlgorithmic use
direct edge$A_{ij}>0$one-step adjacency
walk of length $k$$(A^k)_{ij}>0$fixed-length reachability
any path up to length $n-1$$(I+A+\cdots+A^{n-1})_{ij}>0$transitive closure
In the displayed DAG, how many distinct directed paths go from $A$ to $E$?

sections.label: 9. Spectral view
sections.title: Spectral graph theory intuition
sections.content:
Spectral Graph Theory Intuition

Spectral graph theory studies graphs through eigenvalues and eigenvectors of matrices such as the adjacency matrix $A$ and Laplacian $L$.

Eigenvector equation
A nonzero vector $v$ is an eigenvector of a matrix $M$ with eigenvalue $\lambda$ if
$$Mv=\lambda v.$$
Laplacian zero mode
For any undirected graph, the constant vector $\mathbf{1}$ satisfies
$$L\mathbf{1}=0.$$
The multiplicity of the eigenvalue $0$ equals the number of connected components.
Quadratic form
$$f^T L f=\sum_{\{i,j\}\in E}(f_i-f_j)^2\ge 0.$$

This identity shows that the Laplacian penalizes disagreement across edges. Smooth functions on a graph vary slowly between adjacent vertices.

Spectral objectGraph meaning
zero eigenvalue of $L$connected components
small nonzero eigenvaluesbottlenecks / weak connectivity
eigenvectors of $A$centrality and recurrent structure
eigenvectors of $L$smooth modes and clustering directions
Spectral pipeline
flowchart LR
  G["Graph"] --> A["Matrix A or L"]
  A --> E["Eigenvalues/eigenvectors"]
  E --> C["Connectivity, centrality, clustering"]
For an undirected graph with two connected components, what is the multiplicity of the Laplacian eigenvalue $0$?

sections.label: 10. Centrality
sections.title: Centrality and PageRank intuition
sections.content:
Centrality and PageRank Intuition

Centrality measures try to formalize the idea that some vertices are more important than others. Different centralities encode different definitions of importance.

CentralityCore ideaMatrix object
degree centralitymany immediate neighborsrow sums of $A$
eigenvector centralityconnected to important vertices$Ax=\lambda x$
PageRankrandom surfer equilibriumstationary distribution of a modified transition matrix
betweennesslies on shortest pathspath computations
Eigenvector centrality
A vertex is central if it is adjacent to central vertices. In matrix form, this recursive idea becomes
$$Ax=\lambda x.$$
PageRank form
A common PageRank model solves
$$r=\alpha P^T r+(1-\alpha)v,$$
where $P$ is a transition matrix, $v$ is a teleportation distribution, and $0<\alpha<1$ is a damping factor.
Centrality idea
flowchart LR
  A((A)) --> H((Hub))
  B((B)) --> H
  C((C)) --> H
  H --> D((D))
  H --> E((E))
Modeling warning
Centrality is not a single universal concept. The right centrality depends on what the edges mean: friendship, influence, money flow, disease transmission, dependency, or probability transition.
In the PageRank equation $r=\alpha P^T r+(1-\alpha)v$, what parameter is usually called the damping factor?

sections.label: 11. Workflows
sections.title: Applied modeling workflow
sections.content:
An Applied Graph-Theory Workflow

Applied graph theory is a modeling process. The diagram, the matrix, and the interpretation must agree.

Graph modeling workflow
flowchart LR
  A["Define entities"] --> B["Define edges"]
  B --> C["Choose directed/undirected"]
  C --> D["Choose weights"]
  D --> E["Build matrix representation"]
  E --> F["Compute graph quantity"]
  F --> G["Interpret in domain language"]
Critical habit
Before computing, state what an edge means. A matrix calculation is only meaningful relative to that modeling decision.
QuestionLikely matrixLikely operation
Who is connected to whom?$A$inspect nonzero entries
How many walks connect vertices?$A$compute $A^k$
How smooth is a signal on the graph?$L$evaluate $f^TLf$
What is reachable?$A$transitive closure
Which vertices are central?$A$ or $P$eigenvector/stationary distribution
Example modeling choice
If the vertices are web pages and an edge $i\to j$ means page $i$ links to page $j$, then a directed graph is appropriate. A row-stochastic transition matrix models a random surfer leaving page $i$ for one of its linked pages.
If edges represent one-way hyperlinks, should the graph usually be directed or undirected?

sections.label: 12. Review
sections.title: Worked review exercises
sections.content:
Worked Review Exercises

This section consolidates the core skills: construct $A$, read degrees, compute simple walk counts, use the Laplacian, and interpret matrix operations.

Exercise A: adjacency matrix
For the path graph $1-2-3-4$, the adjacency matrix is
$$A=\begin{pmatrix} 0&1&0&0\\ 1&0&1&0\\ 0&1&0&1\\ 0&0&1&0 \end{pmatrix}.$$
Exercise B: degree matrix
The degree vector of the path graph $1-2-3-4$ is $(1,2,2,1)$, so
$$D=\operatorname{diag}(1,2,2,1).$$
Exercise C: Laplacian
The Laplacian is
$$L=D-A=\begin{pmatrix} 1&-1&0&0\\ -1&2&-1&0\\ 0&-1&2&-1\\ 0&0&-1&1 \end{pmatrix}.$$
For the path graph $1-2-3-4$, what is the degree of vertex $2$?
For the same path graph, what is $L_{23}$?
Takeaway
Applied graph theory becomes powerful when you can move fluently among the picture, the data model, the matrix representation, the computation, and the interpretation.

quiz.stem: For a simple undirected graph, what does the adjacency-matrix entry $a_{ij}=1$ mean?
quiz.feedback: In a simple adjacency matrix, $a_{ij}=1$ records adjacency between vertices $i$ and $j$.

quiz.stem: The adjacency matrix of an undirected graph is symmetric.
quiz.feedback: If $i$ is adjacent to $j$, then $j$ is adjacent to $i$, so $A=A^T$.

quiz.stem: For an unweighted graph, what does $(A^k)_{ij}$ count?
quiz.feedback: Matrix powers count walks: $(A^k)_{ij}$ is the number of $k$-step walks from $i$ to $j$.

quiz.stem: The combinatorial Laplacian of an undirected graph is
The diagonal entries of the degree matrix are the vertex degrees.
For an oriented incidence matrix using the convention in the lesson, the tail of an edge receives which sign?
quiz.feedback: In this convention, the tail receives $-1$ and the head receives $+1$.

quiz.stem: Which matrix expression factors the Laplacian using the oriented incidence matrix $B$?
quiz.feedback: For an undirected graph with oriented incidence matrix $B$, $L=BB^T$.

quiz.stem: A directed adjacency matrix must always be symmetric.
quiz.feedback: Directed edges are asymmetric, so a directed adjacency matrix is generally not symmetric.

quiz.stem: A transition matrix for a Markov chain usually has rows that sum to
quiz.feedback: A row-stochastic transition matrix has each row sum equal to $1$.

quiz.stem: The multiplicity of the Laplacian eigenvalue $0$ equals the number of
quiz.feedback: The zero eigenvalue of the graph Laplacian detects connected components.

quiz.stem: Which centrality is naturally associated with an equation of the form $Ax=\lambda x$?
quiz.feedback: Eigenvector centrality assigns high scores to vertices connected to high-scoring vertices.

quiz.stem: Before using a graph matrix, one should state what vertices and edges mean in the application domain.
quiz.feedback: The interpretation of the matrix depends on the modeling meaning assigned to vertices and edges.
